text: The surgeon enters the abdomen through an incision in the left subcostal region using an optical viewing technique, with six 5mm ports placed for laparoscopic access.

The surgeon identifies the cecum and terminal ileum in the right lower quadrant and traces the small bowel retrograde until encountering abnormal adhesions between loops of small bowel and the mesentery. The small bowel loop is reflected towards the right abdomen, revealing an abnormal fusion of the left mesocolon and small bowel mesentery. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the left mesocolon from the small bowel mesentery.

The surgeon lyses adhesions to reveal the fossa of Lanzert, exposing the hernia sac containing multiple loops of small bowel. The loops are carefully reduced from the hernia sac, with the fossa located left of the fourth portion of the duodenum and posterior to the left mesocolon. The edge of the left mesocolon is visible, containing the inferior mesenteric vein. After reduction, the surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz by running the small bowel retrograde.

The surgeon begins the repair of the hernia by closing the hernia orifice in two layers. The first layer involves a purse string silk suture that approximates the small bowel mesentery, proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon. The second layer uses a running silk suture to reinforce the approximation of the proximal jejunum, mesocolon, and transverse colon.
